title: Enroll in health insurance
description: Review your new employer's health plan options carefully — deductibles, copays, networks, and HSA/FSA eligibility. You usually have 30 days from your start date to enroll.

title: Enroll in dental and vision insurance
description: These are often separate elections. Dental and vision plans are usually inexpensive through an employer and can save you significantly on routine care.

title: Set up your 401(k) or retirement plan
description: Contribute at least enough to get the full employer match — it's free money. Review the fund options and consider a target-date fund if you're unsure where to invest.

title: Understand your vesting schedule
description: Employer 401(k) matches and stock grants often vest over time. Know your vesting schedule so you understand exactly what you'd forfeit if you left early.

title: Review life and disability insurance options
description: Many employers offer basic life insurance for free and supplemental coverage at group rates. Long-term disability insurance is often overlooked but protects your most valuable asset — your income.

title: Enroll in HSA or FSA if available
description: If your health plan qualifies for an HSA, it's one of the best tax-advantaged accounts available — triple tax benefit. An FSA is use-it-or-lose-it but still saves you money on medical expenses.

title: Set up direct deposit
description: Provide your bank routing and account numbers to payroll. Consider splitting your deposit to automatically route a portion to savings.

title: Roll over your old 401(k)
description: Don't leave your old retirement funds behind. Roll them into your new employer's plan or into an IRA to keep things consolidated and avoid potential fees on a dormant account.

title: Update your tax withholdings (W-4)
description: Fill out your W-4 accurately to avoid a big tax bill or an unnecessarily large refund. Use the IRS withholding estimator if your situation is complex.

title: Review your new compensation package in full
description: Beyond salary, understand your total compensation: bonuses, equity, benefits, PTO, professional development budget, commuter benefits, and any other perks. Know what you've got.

title: Update your budget for the new income
description: If your pay changed, adjust your budget. Allocate raises intentionally — increase retirement contributions, pay down debt, or boost savings before lifestyle inflation kicks in.

title: Update LinkedIn and professional profiles
description: Update your job title, company, and start date on LinkedIn. A short post about your new role can surface valuable connections and signal availability to your new network.

title: Schedule introductory meetings with key colleagues
description: Proactively set up 1-on-1s with your manager, direct teammates, and cross-functional partners in your first two weeks. Ask about their role, priorities, and how you can support them.

title: Clarify expectations with your manager
description: In your first week, have a direct conversation about what success looks like in 30, 60, and 90 days. Understand priorities, communication preferences, and how your performance will be evaluated.

title: Learn the tools and systems
description: Get access to all necessary tools — email, Slack, project management, code repos, documentation, HR portal. The faster you're set up, the faster you can contribute.

title: Find a mentor or onboarding buddy
description: Ask your manager or HR if there's a formal buddy program. If not, identify someone who's been at the company long enough to help you navigate the culture and unwritten rules.

title: Complete all onboarding paperwork
description: Handle I-9, tax forms, emergency contacts, direct deposit, and any compliance training promptly. Falling behind on admin tasks can delay your first paycheck or benefits enrollment.

title: Set up your workspace and equipment
description: Whether in-office or remote, get your workstation dialed in — monitor, keyboard, chair ergonomics. Request any equipment you need early; IT procurement can take time.

title: Understand PTO and leave policies
description: Know how PTO accrues, blackout dates, and the process for requesting time off. If you have pre-planned vacations, communicate them to your manager during the first week.

title: Review the employee handbook
description: Skim the employee handbook for policies on remote work, expense reimbursement, professional development, code of conduct, and anything else that affects your day-to-day.

title: Plan your commute
description: Do a test run of your commute before your first day. Check if your employer offers commuter benefits like transit passes, parking subsidies, or bike-to-work programs.

title: Update your professional wardrobe if needed
description: Observe the dress code during interviews or ask HR. Having a few appropriate outfits ready for your first week removes one source of first-day stress.

title: Set up a new routine
description: Adjust your morning routine, meal prep, and exercise schedule around your new work hours. Building sustainable habits early prevents burnout and makes the transition smoother.

title: Handle any non-compete or IP agreements
description: Read any non-compete, non-solicitation, or intellectual property agreements carefully before signing. Understand what's restricted and consult a lawyer if anything seems overly broad.
